questions.question: How did the thieves manage to stop the truck?
choices: They blocked the road with another truck.
They waited for it at the factory gate.
They pretended to be police officers.
They followed it until it stopped for fuel.
questions.explanation: The article says the thieves looked exactly like police officers, so the driver stopped. But they were not real police.

questions.question: What was special about the stolen KitKat bars?
choices: They were shaped like small racing cars.
They were the biggest bars Nestlé has ever made.
They were made with a new kind of chocolate.
They were sold only in Italy and Poland.
questions.explanation: The article explains that every bar was a special edition shaped like a small Formula 1 racing car, made for two big birthdays.

questions.question: Who do the Italian police think stole the truck?
choices: A few hungry men who wanted free chocolate.
Workers from the chocolate factory.
Real police officers from another town.
Organized groups of experienced criminals.
questions.explanation: The article says the police do not believe it was a few hungry men. They are looking at organized crime groups that have robbed trucks before.

questions.question: What happened to the truck driver after the robbery?
choices: He drove away together with the thieves.
He was found safe later.
He hid inside the truck until help came.
He is still missing, like the chocolate.
questions.explanation: The section "Fake Police, Real Thieves" says the driver was found safe later, which was the best news of the whole story.

questions.question: Why did many people think the robbery was a joke?
choices: Nestlé often tells funny stories in the news.
The story sounded like a scene from a movie.
The news appeared just before April Fools' Day.
The number of stolen bars kept changing.
questions.explanation: The article says the news came out just before April 1, a day when companies love to tell funny stories that are not true, so people refused to believe it.

questions.question: How can people check if their KitKat bar is one of the stolen ones?
choices: By entering the code from the wrapper on a website.
By calling the Italian police and describing the bar.
By comparing the bar's shape with photos online.
By taking the bar back to a Nestlé factory.
questions.explanation: The article describes the "Stolen KitKat Tracker": if you scan or type the eight-digit code from the wrapper, the website tells you if the bar is stolen.

questions.question: In the article, the word "heist" means...
choices: a funny story that is not true.
a truck that carries food across Europe.
a special product made in small numbers.
a big robbery that was planned carefully.
questions.explanation: The first section explains the name: newspapers called it "The Great KitKat Heist," which means a big, carefully planned robbery.

questions.question: In the article, the word "wrapper" means...
choices: a person who reports a crime to the police.
the paper around the chocolate.
the eight-digit code on each bar.
a tool on the company's website.
questions.explanation: The section about the tracker explains that the code is on the wrapper — the paper around the chocolate.
